network.overall_strategy: The synthesis network centers on lactaldehyde as a key hub precursor, converted via Strecker synthesis to threonine aminonitrile and hydrolyzed to threonine. Pathways draw from UV cyanosulfidic photoredox (surface), spark-discharge simulations (surface volcanic plumes), HCN oligomerization via aminomalononitrile (surface), and limited hydrothermal precursor generation (e.g., H2S, acetaldehyde), with interconnection: hydrothermal H2S feeds surface photoredox; surface products assemble biochemically. Multiple convergent routes to lactaldehyde and threonine aminonitrile ensure redundancy, grounded in literature routes emphasizing racemic threonine/allothreonine production.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to glycolonitrile
reactions.mechanism: HCN trimerization on clay surfaces to form glycolonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization yields glycolonitrile as a key nitrile intermediate (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: UV photoredox reduction of glycolonitrile to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: UV 254 nm, 2 h irradiation, 10 mM glycolonitrile, 30 mM H2S

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction using Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycling and H2S as reductant to convert nitrile to aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Yields 42% glycolaldehyde from glycolonitrile under UV photoredox (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Acetaldehyde formation via hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch
reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type hydrogenation of methane-derived CO to acetaldehyde on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal vents produce simple aldehydes like acetaldehyde (speculative connection to Strecker precursors; Andersson & Holm, 2000).

reactions.name: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin formation
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of HCN to acetaldehyde, catalyzed by UV on TiO2.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanohydrin formation as precursor to lactaldehyde in cyanosulfidic networks (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: UV photoredox reduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of cyanohydrin to hydroxy aldehyde using H2S and CuCN.
reactions.reasoning: 19% yield of lactaldehyde from acetaldehyde cyanohydrin (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Green et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde to threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Imine formation from aldehyde and NH3, followed by HCN addition to aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Lactaldehyde converts to threonine aminonitrile via Strecker-type reaction (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; meteoritic Strecker, Kyushu report).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of nitrile group to carboxylic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Standard Strecker hydrolysis yields amino acids including threonine (Thanassi, 1975; meteoritic Strecker).

reactions.name: Spark discharge synthesis of threonine precursors

conditions.other: electric discharge simulating volcanic plumes, racemic products

reactions.mechanism: Radical chemistry produces HCN, aldehydes, leading to Strecker-like assembly to threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Spark discharge yields threonine at ~10^-2 relative to glycine (Parker et al., 2011; Ring et al., 1972).

reactions.name: AMN reaction with lactaldehyde to threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Electrophilic addition of AMN to aldehyde, forming aminonitrile intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: AMN + aldehydes yield threonine aminonitriles after hydrolysis (Thanassi, 1975).

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox via glycolonitrile
pathways.description: Surface UV reduction from HCN to glycolaldehyde, branching to acetaldehyde cyanohydrin then lactaldehyde, Strecker to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde feed to photoredox
pathways.description: Hydrothermal production of acetaldehyde, surface cyanohydrin and UV reduction to lactaldehyde, then Strecker.

pathways.name: Direct cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker
pathways.description: Surface photoredox directly to lactaldehyde via acetaldehyde cyanohydrin, immediate Strecker assembly.

pathways.name: Spark discharge direct to threonine
pathways.description: Surface volcanic plume simulation producing threonine via radical/Strecker intermediates.

pathways.name: AMN oligomer route via lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Surface HCN to AMN, reaction with photoredox lactaldehyde to aminonitrile, hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal H2S enhanced photoredox
pathways.description: Hydrothermal H2S feeds surface UV reduction of glycolonitrile path to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark discharge with AMN integration
pathways.description: Spark produces HCN and aldehydes, oligomer to AMN, then to threonine.

pathways.name: Surface Strecker from hydrothermal precursors
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde to surface Strecker via cyanohydrin without full photoredox.

pathways.name: Extended cyanosulfidic with glycolaldehyde branch
pathways.description: Glycolaldehyde as side product, but converges via acetaldehyde to lactaldehyde Strecker.

pathways.name: AMN direct from spark HCN
pathways.description: Spark discharge HCN oligomerizes to AMN, reacts with endogenous lactaldehyde to threonine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong alignment with cyanosulfidic photoredox literature via glycolonitrile reduction; assumes implicit branch from glycolaldehyde to acetaldehyde precursors, which is plausible but not explicit.
suggestions: Explicitly link glycolaldehyde to acetaldehyde formation for clarity.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good hydrothermal-to-surface flow with H2S interconnection; Fischer-Tropsch for acetaldehyde is plausible but less directly supported in literature for H2S/CH4 specifically.
suggestions: Cite specific hydrothermal catalysis for acetaldehyde to strengthen feasibility.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct surface Strecker from lactaldehyde aligns well; assumes prior lactaldehyde availability, coherent with photoredox but lacks upstream detail.
suggestions: Specify upstream lactaldehyde source for completeness.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent match to spark-discharge literature producing threonine directly; includes key gases like H2S, NH3, CH4.
suggestions: Note racemic threonine/allothreonine as per literature.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: AMN route via lactaldehyde supported by Thanassi literature; good convergence but AMN accumulation selectivity is a noted challenge.
suggestions: Address AMN polymerization competition in feedback.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Enhances photoredox with hydrothermal H2S; coherent flow but mixes glycol and acet paths slightly.
Integrates spark with AMN; plausible but assumes HCN from spark feeds AMN.
suggestions: Explicitly link spark HCN to rxn_009.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Surface Strecker from hydrothermal precursors; skips cyanohydrin reduction, assuming direct Strecker on acetaldehyde, which is less specific for threonine.
suggestions: Include lactaldehyde intermediate for threonine specificity.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Extended cyanosulfidic with glycol branch; comprehensive but longer path increases complexity.
AMN direct from spark HCN; good but lacks lactaldehyde detail in AMN reaction.
suggestions: Specify aldehyde input for AMN step.

evaluation.network_feedback: Well-connected network with strong literature alignment to photoredox, spark, and AMN routes; excellent hub use of lactaldehyde and aminonitrile; minor concerns on hydrothermal acetaldehyde specificity, but overall chemically sound and convergent.
